name: Ka'ab ibn Malik
epithet: The One Who Told the Truth
title: Ka'ab ibn Malik (RA): The One Who Told the Truth
meta_description: The story of Ka'ab ibn Malik (RA): poet, warrior of Uhud, and the man whose long repentance after Tabuk became the most famous account of returning to Allah through the truth.
keywords: Ka'ab ibn Malik, story of Kaab ibn Malik, repentance in Islam, tawbah, the three who stayed behind, Tabuk, sahaba stories
lede: He had stood with the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ when it cost everything: at the secret pledge in Makkah, in the worst hour of Uhud, in battle after battle. Then, one summer, he stayed home for no good reason at all.

This is the story of Ka'ab ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him): a poet and a soldier whose single mistake, and the way he answered for it, became the most famous account of repentance in the whole of our history.

sections.h2: A poet from the first believers of Medina
paras: Ka'ab ibn Malik was one of the earliest people of Medina to accept Islam. Both his parents had died before the message reached the city, and he brought his two brothers, Sahl and Abdullah, into faith with him. He was there on the night of the second pledge at Aqabah, when a small group from Medina slipped into Makkah and gave their allegiance to a man his own people had cast out.

He was one of the three poets of the Prophet ﷺ. His was war poetry: verse of strength and courage that he carried onto the battlefield to lift the spirits of the believers. It was said that some who came to fight heard the confidence in his words and laid down their arms before a single blow was struck.

When the believers of Makkah and Medina were paired as brothers, the Prophet ﷺ joined Ka'ab with az-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam (may Allah be pleased with him), his own close companion. To be chosen as the brother of such a man told everyone, from the very beginning, the standing that Ka'ab held.

He never forgot that night at Aqabah. People spoke of Badr for the rest of their lives, but Ka'ab used to say he would not trade the night of the pledge for the day of Badr, famous as Badr was. To slip into Makkah in secret, with no safety at all, and give his word to a man hunted by his own people: that, he said, was the most beloved night of his life.

sections.h2: The day he would not leave the Prophet's side
paras: Ka'ab joined every major expedition with the Prophet ﷺ but two. On the day of Uhud, when the Muslims were struck from behind and a rumour spread that the Prophet ﷺ had been killed, the field fell into confusion and despair. Ka'ab was the first to catch sight of him alive, and he called out the good news so the believers would not lose heart.

Then he did something almost no one would. He swapped his own armour with the Prophet ﷺ, drawing the enemy toward himself and away from him. He carried seventeen serious wounds out of that day. He and az-Zubayr, both badly hurt, held each other up and kept fighting.
This is why his later mistake stung so deeply. A man who had given that much, who had stepped in front of danger for the Prophet ﷺ, was not the kind of man anyone expected to fall short.

The Quran does not speak gently of poets, and this worried Ka'ab. He came to the Prophet ﷺ and said that Allah had revealed about the poets what He had revealed, as if to ask whether the gift he used was a thing to be ashamed of.

The Prophet ﷺ answered him plainly. The one who strives, he said, strives with his sword and with his tongue. And he told Ka'ab that the words he sent against the enemy were like arrows loosed at them. His poetry was not a lesser kind of service. It was service.

On another day the Prophet ﷺ recalled a verse Ka'ab had once composed and said that Allah had not forgotten it, for Allah does not forget any good a person does. When Ka'ab asked which words he meant, the Prophet ﷺ turned to Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with him), who recited the lines back from memory. It was a quiet honour, and a reminder that no good word is ever truly lost.
Years passed. The call came to march on Tabuk: a long road in the burning heat, through land with no water, toward a far larger army. The Prophet ﷺ told the people to prepare, and to keep preparing.

Ka'ab meant to go. But for the first time in his life he had a little ease, two camels and a garden whose fruit was ripening, and he told himself he would get ready when the time came. He went out one morning and came back having done nothing. He would go tomorrow, he thought. Then one morning the army was simply gone, and he had missed it.

He walked out into a Medina emptied of its best, left among the openly insincere and those excused by illness. Forty years later, blind and old, telling the story to his son, he still paused in the middle of it to say plainly: I wish I had gone.

sections.h2: Nothing would save me but the truth
paras: When word came that the Prophet ﷺ was returning, Ka'ab thought of all the excuses he could give. He was a poet; he knew how to soften the truth. But the closer the return came, the clearer it grew to him that no story would save him. Only the truth would.

The Prophet ﷺ reached Medina, prayed two units in the mosque, and sat. Around eighty men came forward with their excuses, and he accepted them at face value and left their hearts to Allah. Then it was Ka'ab's turn. The Prophet ﷺ smiled the smile of a man holding back anger and asked what had kept him back.

Ka'ab answered honestly. "By Allah," he said, "if I were sitting before anyone else, I could talk my way out of his anger. But I know that if I lie to you today to please you, Allah will soon make you angry with me. And if I tell you the truth that angers you, I can hope for a good end from Allah." He had no excuse, he admitted. He had never been stronger or better able to go, and he simply had not. The Prophet ﷺ said he had told the truth, and told him to wait for Allah to decide his case.

sections.h2: Fifty nights, and the test that came with them
paras: Two other sincere men, Murarah ibn ar-Rabi and Hilal ibn Umayyah, had done the same and refused to lie. The Prophet ﷺ forbade the Muslims from speaking to the three of them. The city Ka'ab loved turned silent around him. He greeted people and they would not answer. He prayed near the Prophet ﷺ and watched to see whether his lips moved in reply. When he asked his own dear cousin, Abu Qatadah, whether he knew that Ka'ab loved Allah and His Messenger, the answer was only silence, and then: "Allah and His Messenger know best." It broke him.

Into that loneliness came a letter from a foreign king: leave the man who has abandoned you, come to us, and we will honour you. Ka'ab saw it at once for what it was, another test, and he took it to the oven and burned it.

On the fiftieth night, after the dawn prayer, sitting on his roof with the wide earth feeling too tight to hold him, he heard a voice carry across the hills: rejoice, Ka'ab, rejoice. He fell at once into prostration. Allah had accepted his repentance, and the Prophet ﷺ, his face shining like the moon, told him: rejoice in the best day of your life since the day your mother bore you.

ayah.text: And to the three men who stayed behind: when the earth, for all its spaciousness, closed in around them, when their very souls closed in around them, when they realized that the only refuge from God was with Him, He turned to them in mercy in order for them to return [to Him]. God is the Ever Relenting, the Most Merciful.

sections.h2: A tongue that never lied again
paras: In his joy, Ka'ab offered to give away all his wealth as thanks. The Prophet ﷺ told him gently to keep some of it, that this was better for him. So Ka'ab kept only what he had at Khaybar and gave the rest for the sake of Allah.

Then he made a vow that would shape the rest of his days. Allah had saved him through honesty, so he resolved never to tell a lie again as long as he lived. He used to say that he knew of no one more severely tested for telling the truth than himself, and that no blessing in his life was greater than that one truth he had spoken before the Prophet ﷺ.

Allah confirmed all of it in the Quran: He had turned to the three who stayed behind, and He counted them among the truthful. Ka'ab lived a long and peaceful life after this, going blind in his old age, his sons leading him to the mosque where people came to ask him about the days he had seen.

He stayed clear of wrongdoing to the end. When unrest later reached its worst and Uthman (may Allah be pleased with him) was besieged, Ka'ab refused to take any part in the bloodshed, and grieved at the abandonment of a righteous man. The one who had learned the cost of a single mistake spent the rest of his days being careful with his deeds and his words.

ayah.text: You who believe, be mindful of God: stand with those who are true.

lessons_intro: His story is not really about a battle he missed. It is about the moment he chose, with everything to lose, to tell the truth.

lessons.bold: The truth costs less than it seems.
lessons.text: A lie would have ended his trial in a minute. The truth took fifty hard nights, and then it lasted forever. He never regretted which one he chose.

lessons.bold: Small delays grow into great regret.
lessons.text: He did not refuse the call. He just put it off, one comfortable day at a time, until the moment was gone. Forty years later it still ached.

lessons.bold: Owning your mistake is the way back.
lessons.text: He did not blame his garden or his camels or the heat. He sat before the Prophet ﷺ and said plainly: I had no excuse. That honesty was the door Allah opened.

lessons.bold: Hold fast when the test arrives in disguise.
lessons.text: At his lowest, an easier life was offered to him by those who wished him harm. He recognised it and burned it, and trusted that relief would come from Allah alone.

faq.a: A companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ from Medina, one of the first believers there and one of his three poets. He fought at Uhud and most other expeditions, and is best known for the account of his repentance after staying behind from Tabuk.

faq.q: Why did Ka'ab ibn Malik stay behind from Tabuk?
faq.a: He had no real excuse. For the first time he had some wealth and a ripening garden, and he kept putting off his preparations until the army left without him. He admitted this openly to the Prophet ﷺ rather than offer a false excuse.

faq.q: What happened to Ka'ab after Tabuk?
faq.a: The Prophet ﷺ instructed the Muslims not to speak to him and two others for fifty nights, until Allah revealed the acceptance of their repentance in the Quran. Ka'ab then vowed never to lie again, and lived a long life as a respected narrator of the Prophet's life.

faq.q: What can we learn from Ka'ab ibn Malik?
faq.a: That honesty is worth its cost, that small delays can lead to lasting regret, and that the surest way back to Allah is to own our mistakes truthfully rather than excuse them.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us will never miss an army marching to Tabuk. But all of us know the smaller version of Ka'ab's mistake: the thing we meant to do, kept putting off, and let slip away while life felt comfortable. And all of us know the moment afterward, when it would be so easy to reach for an excuse.

Ka'ab's life leaves us one quiet question for the day ahead. When the truth is harder than the lie, which one will we choose? He chose the truth, carried its weight, and was never sorry. That choice is still open to every one of us, in the ordinary moments no one else may ever see.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Ka'ab ibn Malik (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Quran translations are from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 9:118 and 9:119). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

He carried seventeen wounds off that field.

Years later, the call came to march on Tabuk.

Ka'ab meant to go. But he had a little ease for the first time, a garden, two camels.

"I'll get ready when the time comes," he told himself.

Then the army was gone.

When the Prophet ﷺ returned, eighty men came with excuses, and he accepted them.

Ka'ab thought of his own. He was a poet; he knew how to soften the truth.

Then he decided: nothing would save him but the truth.

"By Allah, I had no excuse. I have never been stronger or better able to go."

The Prophet ﷺ said he had told the truth, and told him to wait for Allah to decide.

For fifty nights, no one in Medina would speak to him.

Even his beloved cousin fell silent.

A foreign king wrote: leave him, come to us, we will honour you.

Ka'ab burned the letter.

On the fiftieth night, a voice carried across the hills:

"Rejoice, Ka'ab, rejoice."

Allah had accepted his repentance. The Prophet ﷺ, his face like the moon, called it the best day of his life.

**The truth costs less than it seems.**

A lie would have ended his trial in a minute. The truth lasted forever, and he never once regretted it.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
Ka'ab ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) had stood with the Prophet ﷺ in the hardest moments: the secret pledge at Aqabah, and the day of Uhud, where he swapped armour with the Prophet ﷺ and carried seventeen wounds.

Then came Tabuk, and for once he had a little comfort. He kept putting off his preparations until the army left without him. He had no real excuse, and when the Prophet ﷺ returned, he refused to invent one.

For fifty nights no one in Medina would speak to him. He chose to bear it rather than lie. And when Allah finally announced his forgiveness in the Quran, he vowed never to tell a lie again as long as he lived.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The most famous repentance in our history
email.alt_subject: Fifty nights of silence, and the truth that ended them
email.preview: He could have given an easy excuse. He chose the harder, truer thing.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Ka'ab ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) was not a man you would expect to fall short. He was one of the first believers of Medina, one of the three poets of the Prophet ﷺ, and on the day of Uhud he swapped his own armour with the Prophet ﷺ to draw the enemy away from him, carrying seventeen wounds off the field.

Then came the march to Tabuk. For the first time in his life Ka'ab had a little ease: a garden whose fruit was ripening, two camels. He meant to go. But he kept putting off his preparations, one comfortable day at a time, until the morning he woke to find the army already gone.

When the Prophet ﷺ returned, around eighty men came forward with excuses, and he accepted them. Ka'ab was a poet; he knew how to soften the truth. But he decided that nothing would save him except honesty.

So he sat before the Prophet ﷺ and said it plainly: "By Allah, I had no excuse. I have never been stronger or better able to go than I was then." The Prophet ﷺ said he had told the truth, and told him to wait for Allah's decision.

What followed was hard. For fifty nights, the Muslims were instructed not to speak to him. The city he loved went silent. Even his closest cousin would not answer his greeting. A foreign king wrote offering him honour if he would abandon the Muslims; Ka'ab burned the letter.

Then, on the fiftieth night, a voice carried across the hills: rejoice. Allah had accepted his repentance and named him, in the Quran, among the truthful. Ka'ab gave much of his wealth in thanks, and made a vow he kept for the rest of his long life: never to tell a lie again.

There is a quiet question in his story for our own day. When the truth is harder than the lie, which one will we choose? He chose the truth, carried its weight, and was never sorry.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Ka'ab ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) was among the first people of Medina to believe. He was one of the three poets of the Prophet ﷺ, and a soldier who did not flinch.

On the day of Uhud, when a rumour spread that the Prophet ﷺ had been killed and the believers lost heart, Ka'ab was the first to see him alive and call out the good news. Then he traded his own armour for the Prophet's, drawing the enemy toward himself, and carried seventeen wounds away from that field.

Years later the call came to march on Tabuk, across a long and waterless road in the summer heat. Ka'ab meant to go. But for once he had a little comfort, a garden and two camels, and he told himself he would prepare when the time came. He delayed, one easy day after another, until the morning he woke and the whole army was gone.

When the Prophet ﷺ returned, many men came with excuses, and he accepted them. Ka'ab was a poet who could have done the same. Instead he chose the truth. "By Allah," he said, "I had no excuse. I have never been better able to go than I was then."

For that honesty he was tested. For fifty nights no one in Medina would speak to him. His own beloved cousin fell silent. A foreign king sent a letter offering him honour if he would leave the Muslims, and Ka'ab carried it to the oven and burned it.

On the fiftieth night, sitting on his roof while the wide earth felt too tight to hold him, he heard a voice across the hills: rejoice, Ka'ab, rejoice. He fell into prostration. Allah had accepted his repentance and named him, in the Quran, among the truthful.

He gave much of his wealth in thanks, and vowed never to tell a lie again as long as he lived. He kept that vow into his old age, when he had gone blind and his sons would lead him to the mosque, and he never stopped saying that the truth was the greatest blessing of his life.
storybook.pullquote: Nothing would save me but the truth.
storybook.reflection: Ka'ab's mistake was small and very human: he meant to act, and kept putting it off. What made him remarkable was what came after. When an easy lie was within reach, he chose the harder truth and carried its weight for fifty silent nights. Owning our mistakes honestly, instead of excusing them, is still the surest way back.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
